abstract:
Enzymatic heme and non-heme systems utilize high-valent iron-oxo species to activate strong C–H bonds with remarkable efficiency, yet replicating this reactivity in synthetic models remains a significant challenge. The primary obstacles include the inherent instability of the terminal iron-oxo moiety and the difficulty of achieving the high-spin ($S = 2$) ground state typically found in nature’s non-heme iron(IV)-oxo intermediates. Most synthetic mononuclear analogues are limited by steric bulk or electronic environments that favor lower spin states.

In this work, we demonstrate that these challenges can be overcome using metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), which provide site-isolated iron centers within a constrained, weak-field ligand environment. We show that the framework $\text{Fe}_2(\text{dobdc})$ and its magnesium-diluted variant, $\text{Fe}_{0.1}\text{Mg}_{1.9}(\text{dobdc})$, effectively convert ethane to ethanol and acetaldehyde using nitrous oxide as the terminal oxidant under mild conditions. Characterization of the reaction products and decay pathways, supported by electronic structure calculations, suggests that the active oxidant is a transient, high-spin ($S = 2$) iron(IV)-oxo species. These findings highlight the utility of MOFs as versatile scaffolds for stabilizing and interrogating highly reactive intermediates that are otherwise elusive in molecular chemistry.
